attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old man is being reviewed by a rehabilitation physician for an assessment of suitability to drive following an ischaemic stroke one month ago. Deficits at the time of the stroke included left sided upper limb weakness, left sided facial muscle weakness and left homonymous hemianopia.

Visual assessment one month post stroke exhibits 6/6 visual acuity bilaterally, normal visual fields on confrontation, appropriate ocular movements without complains of diplopia and no evidence of visual neglect or inattention. Further physical assessment is significant for 4/5 weakness of the left hand with normal strength of remaining movements, sensation and proprioception. A Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) is conducted and produces a score of 28/30 (normal being greater than 26/30). The patient works in an administrative role and drives a private, automatic transmission vehicle.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Do not reinstate driver's license and review again in 2 months' time
attributes.explanation:
This patient has had an ischaemic stroke four weeks ago, and has residual left hand 4/5 weakness but otherwise has made a full recovery. A muscle strength rating of 4/5 signifies that this patient can activate their left hand muscles against some resistance and have full range of motion, but not against the examiners full resistance. This can be a common finding in certain population groups. As the patient in the stem had previously suffered from a homonymous hemianopia it is strongly recommended that a formal perimetry based assessment such as a Humphrey visual field assessment to ensure there are no residual visual field defects and meet the visual driving standards.

The medical standards for licensing of a private drivers licence indicate that post-stroke, patients should not drive for at least four weeks and the cause of the stroke should be identified and managed. Additionally, to receive an unconditional licence again, an appropriate specialist must provide an analysis regarding if there are any significant impairments of: visuospatial perception, insight, judgement, attention, comprehension, reaction time, memory, sensation, muscle power, coordination, vision, and the likely impact any deficit may have on driving ability.

If the patient were to have persistent significant impairments related to the above areas then a GP may refer for a practical driving assessment. A practical driving assessment will assess the impact of the stroke in relation to driving skills and is typically referred to when clinical assessment shows a borderline case of whether a patient is fit to drive. It can also be utilised to determine if there is a need for vehicle modifications. As significant impairments are not seen in this case at four weeks post-stroke, a practical driving assessment and vehicle modification are not required.

If this patient was requiring a commercial license (e.g. driving heavy vehicles or public passenger vehicles) then they must not have their licence reinstated for at least three months. After experiencing a stroke, the patient is only eligible for a conditional drivers licence with annual review, and the condition/s will be related to the judgement and analysis of a GP or the results of a practical driver assessment. As this patient is only seeking a private licence, these medical standards are not needing to be implemented.

If the patient were to have permanent significant impairments to the aforementioned areas that impact on their driving ability, then indefinite suspension of a commercial and private licence may occur. This patient does not have any permanent significant impairments that would impact their driving ability, and therefore does not need their licence suspended indefinitely.

This patient has waited four weeks after their stroke to seek reinstatement of their private drivers licence, and they do not have any significant impairments that would affect their driving ability, as assessed by his GP. If he meets the visual standards following formal perimetry based assessment, reinstating their drivers licence would be appropriate.
learningPoints: A post-stroke patient that does not have any significant visual, cognitive or neuronal impairments that would affect driving ability can have their private licence reinstated after 4 weeks, and their a commercial licence reinstated with conditions after 3 months
Patients that have visual, cognitive or neuronal impairments but it is difficult to determine the impairments effects on driving ability should be referred for a practical driver assessment
references: Naqvi U, Sherman Al. Muscle Strength Grading. [Updated 2021 Sep 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436008/

Austroads and National Transport Commission. Assessing Fitness to Drive for Commercial and Private Vehicle Drivers - Medical Standards for Licensing and Clinical Management Guidelines. 2022. Available from URL: https://austroads.com.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0037/498691/AP-G56-22_As...
